solution: Using \( b^{m} \cdot b^{n} = b^{m+n} \) and \( b^{m}/b^{n} = b^{m-n} \), the expression is \( [[b]]^{[[e1]] + [[e2]] - [[e3]]} = [[b]]^{[[E]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the base by the exponent instead of raising the base to that power.
This reports the exponent \( [[E]] \) itself instead of \( [[b]]^{[[E]]} \).
This uses an exponent that is one too large.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the power-of-a-power rule, \( (b^{m})^{n} = b^{mn} \), so this equals \( [[b]]^{[[e1]] \cdot [[e2]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the exponents, as if multiplying two powers, rather than multiplying them as required for a power raised to a power.
This multiplies the base by both exponents instead of raising it to their product.
This adds an extra 1 to the sum of the exponents.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: A negative exponent means take the reciprocal: \( [[b]]^{-[[n]]} = \dfrac{1}{[[b]]^{[[n]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This negates the value instead of taking its reciprocal.
This drops the negative sign entirely, giving a positive power instead of its reciprocal.
This takes the reciprocal but keeps an incorrect negative sign.
This multiplies the base by the exponent in the denominator instead of raising the base to a power.
This is larger than the correct value.
This is double the correct value.

solution: A \( 1/n \) power is the same as an \( n \)-th root. Since \( [[b]] = [[k]]^{[[n]]} \), \( [[b]]^{1/[[n]]} = [[k]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( n \) instead of taking the \( n \)-th root.
This subtracts \( n \) instead of taking the \( n \)-th root.
This uses the wrong exponent on \( k \), one less than needed to recover the base.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the power-of-a-product rule, \( (ab)^{n} = a^{n}b^{n} \), so \( \dfrac{([[a]]\cdot[[b]])^{[[n]]}}{[[a]]^{[[n]]}} = [[b]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to raise \( b \) to the power \( n \), leaving it unraised.
This reports \( a^{n} \) instead of \( b^{n} \), the term that should remain after cancellation.
This uses an exponent one too small on \( b \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: Since the exponent \( 2\cdot[[n]] \) is even and \( [[b]] > 0 \), \( \sqrt{[[b]]^{2\cdot[[n]]}} = [[b]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This halves the whole power instead of halving just the exponent.
This leaves the expression unrooted, forgetting the square root entirely.
This uses an exponent one too small.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: Write both radicals as exponents of \( [[k]] \): \( [[b]] = [[k]]^{6} \), so \( \sqrt{[[b]]} = [[k]]^{3} \) and \( \sqrt[3]{[[b]]} = [[k]]^{2} \). Using a common index (sixths: \( 1/2 + 1/3 = 5/6 \)), the product is \( [[k]]^{3} \cdot [[k]]^{2} = [[k]]^{5} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( b \) itself, forgetting to combine the two different radical indices at all.
This uses the wrong combined exponent, \( 4 \) instead of \( 5 \), on \( k \).
This divides by 6 instead of combining the fractional exponents \( 1/2 \) and \( 1/3 \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This drops the negative sign, giving a positive power of \( k \) instead of its reciprocal.
This has the reciprocal but an incorrect negative sign in front.
This inverts the fractional exponent, using \( n/m \) instead of \( m/n \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
